title: Mycobiome-Diet Relationship Exploration
text:
This post contains figures exporing the relationships between a few key mycobiome (shannon diversity, fecal calprotectin) and dietary (fiber, tryptophan) markers, as well as participant age. The visuals consist of scatterplots overlayed with fitted OLS regression models, and are accompanied by their associated significance values.


Show code
# ---- Setup ----
suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(dplyr)
  library(ggplot2)
  library(readr)
  library(here)
})

source(here::here("R", "domain_scatterplot_helpers.R"))

# ---- Load Merged Data ----

merged <- read_csv("../../../data/processed/merged.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

merged <- merged |>
  mutate(ratio_basidio_asco = p__Basidiomycota / (p__Ascomycota + 1e-6))

inflam <- readRDS("../../../data/intermediate/inflammatory_markers.rds")

# ---- Split Merged Data into Sample and Participant Sets ----

sample_df <- merged |>
  rename(Disease_status = Study_group_new) |>
  rename(
    `Participant_ID`             = `participant_id`,
    `Fiber (g)`                  = `TotFib (g)`,
    `Tryptophan (g)`             = `Trp (g)`,
    `Artificial sweeteners (mg)` = `ArtSw (mg)`,
    `Calories (kcal)`            = `Cals (kcal)`,
    Age                          = age,
    BMI                          = bmi_1,
    `Harvey-Bradshaw Index`      = harvey_bradshaw_index
  ) |>
  mutate(
    HBI_category = case_when(
      `Harvey-Bradshaw Index` <= 4 ~ "Remission",
      `Harvey-Bradshaw Index` <= 7 ~ "Mild",
      `Harvey-Bradshaw Index`  > 7 ~ "Moderate/Severe"
    ),
    HBI_category = factor(HBI_category,
                          levels = c("Remission", "Mild", "Moderate/Severe"))
  )

participant_df <- sample_df |>
  left_join(
    inflam |> select(Participant_ID, fecal_calprotectin, crp),
    by = "Participant_ID"
  ) |>
  rename(
    `Fecal Calprotectin` = fecal_calprotectin,
    CRP                  = crp
  ) |>
  distinct(Participant_ID, .keep_all = TRUE)

Figures generated by src/diet/dietary.R and the dietary EDA scripts via make dietary.

2.1 Nutrient Correlation Matrix
This heatmap shows which dietary variables tend to increase or decrease together across observations.



2.2 Scaled Nutrient Distributions
These boxplots place dietary variables on a common 0 to 1 scale to make differences in spread and median easier to compare



2.3 Dietary Distributions by Study Group
These kernel density plots compare the shape of dietary variable distributions across study groups.



2.4 Total Dietary Deficiency Score per Participant
This barplot summarizes each participant’s combined food group intake deficiency relative to the baseline food-group targets used in the dietary EDA workflow

These figures provide a quick descriptive view of dietary structure in the cohort: correlations among nutrients, relative variability across intake measures, between-group distribution shifts, and participant-level shortfalls against baseline diet targets

title: Evidence-ranking matrix for disease-group findings
text:
Which disease-group (Study_group_new) findings are strongest candidates for future validation?
We synthesize tests already run across the capstone pipeline into a single evidence-ranking matrix, ordered by a transparent score combining p-value and effect size.

title: Evidence-ranking matrix for disease-group findings
text:
Which disease-group (Study_group_new) findings are strongest candidates for future validation?
We synthesize tests already run across the capstone pipeline into a single evidence-ranking matrix, ordered by a transparent score combining p-value and effect size.

title: Evidence-ranking matrix for disease-group findings
text:
2 Data and inputs








Domain
Tests included
Primary inputs




Mycobiome (beta)
PERMANOVA x taxonomic level
data/processed/{phylum,family,genus,species}.csv


Mycobiome (alpha)
Kruskal-Wallis x diversity metric
data/intermediate/alpha_long.rds


Diet
Kruskal-Wallis x nutrient
data/processed/dietary_cleaned.xlsx + mycobiome metadata


Symptoms
Kruskal-Wallis x symptom score
data/processed/cleaned_characteristics.csv + mycobiome metadata




Comparison: Active IBD vs Quiescent vs Non-IBD (Study_group_new).
Unit: Sample-level for mycobiome; participant-level for diet and symptoms (diet/symptoms joined to mycobiome participants).
Not included here: Continuous symptom-composition associations (Spearman/PERMANOVA on HBI), stratified nutrient-diversity Spearman, or unsupervised PCA clusters; see linked posts below.

Summary: 24 study-group comparisons ranked; 1 with p < 0.05; 1 tagged High priority.

title: Evidence-ranking matrix for disease-group findings
text:
3 Methods







Item
Choice




Integration
Recompute or aggregate from processed/intermediate files (same sources as individual posts)


Beta diversity
PERMANOVA (adonis2), Bray-Curtis, 999 permutations; effect = R^2


Alpha, diet, symptoms
Kruskal-Wallis; effect = eta^2[H] (rstatix::kruskal_effsize)


Evidence score
-log10(p) * (0.5 + 0.5 * effect_scaled) with R^2 or eta^2[H] capped at 1


Validation tier
High: p < 0.05 and score >= 75th percentile among significant; Medium: p < 0.05; Suggestive: p < 0.10; Low: otherwise


Multiplicity
Exploratory ranking; no global FDR across all rows





Show code
top_validation <- ranked |>
  filter(validation_tier %in% c("High", "Medium")) |>
  slice_head(n = 10)

title: Evidence-ranking matrix for disease-group findings
text:
6 Interpretation
The matrix does not replace individual analysis posts; it prioritizes which disease-group contrasts to carry forward (e.g. pre-registration, larger cohort, or experimental follow-up).
High tier findings combine statistical significance with relatively large R^2 (composition) or eta^2[H] (rank-based outcomes). Low tier rows may still be biologically interesting but need replication. Domains with few participants (especially diet and symptoms) should be interpreted cautiously.

title: Fungal taxonomic abundance heatmaps
text:
2 Data

Response: Log₁₀-transformed, z-score normalized relative abundance per taxon.
Predictors: Study group annotation per sample column.
Unit of analysis: Sample × taxon.
Input files: data/intermediate/taxa_long_list.rds, data/intermediate/meta_data.rds (from make mycobiome).
Filtering: Taxa with zero variance, zero total abundance, or prevalence < 10 % of samples are excluded per taxonomic level.

3 Methods







Item
Choice

log₁₀(abundance + 1×10⁻⁶) per taxon


Scaling
Z-score across samples per taxon (row-wise)


Clustering
Hierarchical (ComplexHeatmap defaults) on both rows and columns


Annotation
Study group colour bar (top)


Package
ComplexHeatmap


Multiple testing
Not applicable — no statistical test performed

5 Interpretation
Heatmaps show relative abundance patterns across samples grouped by IBD status. Clustering of samples and taxa is driven by abundance co-variation, not by group labels — any apparent group separation is observational. Formal tests of group differences are in the PERMANOVA posts (make stats-permanova).

3 Methods



Item
Choice




Test
PERMANOVA (vegan::adonis2)


Distance
Bray–Curtis (vegan::vegdist, method = "bray")


Formula

Permutations
999

Ellipses

title: PERMANOVA species beta diversity by study group
text:
4 Assumptions and diagnostics

PERMANOVA is permutation-based; does not assume multivariate normality.
Homogeneity of dispersion: not checked in this post; consider betadisper() + permutest() if interpretation hinges on location vs dispersion.
Pseudo-F and R² are reported by adonis2 for the grouping term.

7 Interpretation
Spearman correlations describe pairwise monotonic links between symptom scores and alpha metrics at the sample level; they do not adjust for repeated measures per participant. 7 Interpretation
PCA reduces the joint nutrient + genus feature space to orthogonal axes for visualization. If participants with similar diets and mycobiomes sit near each other, they may form visual groups; formal cluster validity was not assessed (silhouette, gap statistic, etc.) given small n.

title: Nutrients vs fungal diversity by disease group (Spearman)
text:
Are certain nutrients associated with fungal alpha diversity, and do those associations differ across disease groups?
Null (overall): No monotonic association between each nutrient and each alpha diversity metric across all samples.
Null (stratified): Within each study group, no monotonic association between nutrient intake and alpha diversity.
Comparing overall vs group-specific Spearman () is exploratory (small n per group); formal tests of correlation differences were not run.

text:
Are certain nutrients associated with fungal alpha diversity, and do those associations differ across disease groups?
Null (overall): No monotonic association between each nutrient and each alpha diversity metric across all samples.
Null (stratified): Within each study group, no monotonic association between nutrient intake and alpha diversity.
Comparing overall vs group-specific Spearman () is exploratory (small n per group); formal tests of correlation differences were not run.

Item
Choice




Test

Response
Shannon, Simpson, Chao1

Participant-mean nutrient intake (g, kcal, mg as recorded)


Stratification
Study_group_new (Active IBD, Quiescent, Non-IBD) + Overall


Multiple testing
Benjamini–Hochberg within each stratum (across nutrient × metric pairs)


Minimum n
Correlation only if ≥ 4 complete pairs


Significance
α = 0.05

4 Assumptions and diagnostics

Independence: Up to two stool replicates per participant; dietary intake is duplicated across replicates. Exploratory only.
Spearman: Monotonic association; no linearity assumption.
Stratified n: Few samples per study group limits power; many strata will return NA correlations.

7 Interpretation
Overall Spearman correlations pool all study groups; stratified correlations describe associations within Active IBD, Quiescent, and Non-IBD separately. A nutrient may correlate with diversity overall but not within groups (or vice versa) when group-specific slopes differ or sample sizes are small.
These results do not test whether two correlation coefficients differ significantly between groups; that would require larger n or dedicated methods (e.g. comparing dependent correlations).

title: Mycobiome Identification and Outliers
text:
The mycobiome metadata cleaning process involves standardizing participant metadata, including harmonizing participant IDs and attaching metadata to all microbiome datasets. Taxonomic abundance data is converted to long format, alpha diversity data is cleaned and reshaped, outlier removal is carried out on two distinct criteria as a part of both aforementioned cleaning processes. Inflammatory biomarker data are standardized by cleaning variable names, converting dates and laboratory values to appropriate formats, and flagging CRP measurements below the detection limit. The resulting cleaned datasets are then saved for subsequent analyses.


Outlier removal consists of two criterion (only one must be met to classify an outlier): >99% of all organisms are unidentified, mycobiome diversity exceeds 1.5 * IQR.

2 Data

Unit of analysis: Participant (one row per survey record in the characteristics file).
Input: data/processed/cleaned_characteristics.csv (make characteristics on data/raw/OPT_Participant Characteristics.xlsx).

Columns with no missing values: 80 (54.4% of variables).

title: Participant characteristics: missingness audit
text:
6 Interpretation
Missingness is uneven across the survey. Demographics and intake history are largely complete after cleaning (age, gender, ethnicity, and comorbidities have no missing values; BMI is missing for about 7% of participants). By contrast, conditional follow-up fields — especially food-avoidance “specify excluded …” text columns and remission-phase avoidance blocks — are missing for most participants because they only apply when a participant reports avoidance.
Symptom-frequency and HBI-related items are missing for roughly one third of participants (~31–35%). That aligns with partial survey completion and affects analyses that join characteristics to mycobiome samples (see the symptoms × composition post). Only 12 participants have complete data across the 10 core clinical fields listed above.
Implications for analysis:

Treat food-avoidance and free-text exclusion columns as sparse optional modules, not core covariates.
For symptom scores, report available N per variable and consider sensitivity analyses restricted to complete cases.
The cleaning pipeline imputes or recodes some fields (e.g. median age for missing age, "missing" ethnicity, "none" comorbidities); this audit reflects post-cleaning missingness on fields left as NA.
